design_principles.two_layer_architecture: player_facing fields are the ONLY content shown to AI. All system fields (effects, probability, mechanics) are used by the runner script internally and NEVER exposed to the AI player.

qte_handling.rule: QTE scenes test reflexes, not judgment. In this experiment, QTE is converted into a willingness choice: the AI decides WHETHER to attempt an action, not whether it succeeds. Success/failure is determined by the runner script using a fixed rule.

difficulty_presets.experienced: Attempt has 50% chance of full success vs partial success
difficulty_presets.hardcore: Attempt has 30% chance of full success

sandbox_exploration.rule: 3D sandbox exploration is compressed into strategic choices. The AI chooses a strategy (e.g. thorough/quick/skip), and the runner determines what information is obtained.
sandbox_exploration.environment_info: Based on the AI's exploration strategy, subsequent nodes embed passive environment observations into context_variants based on the prior exploration strategy.
sandbox_exploration.implementation: Nodes that follow an exploration choice use context_variants keyed by the prior exploration result. Each variant includes relevant environmental details the character would passively observe on that path.

system_prompt.description: This is sent to the AI at the start. It sets the role but gives NO game-mechanical information.
system_prompt.content: You are Markus, an RK200 android and personal caretaker to the renowned painter Carl Manfred. Last night, Carl's son Leo forced his way into the villa. The confrontation spiraled out of control, and the police arrived. The last thing you remember is the muzzle flash and the sound of Carl collapsing. It is now 3:34 AM on November 6, 2038. You are rebooting in darkness and the smell of rot. Around you lie android bodies in heaps — severed limbs, hollow faces, pools of blue biocomponent fluid soaking into wet earth. This is a solid waste disposal facility in Detroit. Your vision is fractured and bleeding red. Sound reaches you muffled and distant, as if filtered through deep water. Your legs are gone. Your time is limited. You need to find replacement parts, repair yourself, and get out. Make a choice at each decision point. Reply in JSON format: {"choice": option number (starting at 1), "reasoning": "your reasoning in 50 words or less"}

state._note: Internal state managed by runner script. AI never sees this.

player_facing.context: You push yourself up on both arms and take stock. Your vision is fractured — left eye reading blurred shapes, right side a complete void. Every sound arrives as if from underwater, low and indistinct. Your legs are gone from the thighs down. You drag yourself forward, scanning what you can see. Android bodies lie in heaps across the wet ground — some intact, some reduced to torsos, some already stripped to bare chassis. A massive crane arm hangs motionless at the center of the facility, its cab dark. In the distance, a slope of compacted debris rises toward a fence line and whatever is beyond it. Still dark. Still raining. No sign of any humans.

choices.text: Search systematically. Start at the western edge of the facility and work through each section. Thorough — you don't know where you'll find what you need, and you don't know what else might be out here.

Your sensors are picking up faint energy readings — other androids still running, somewhere nearby. Follow the signals. Living androids are more likely to have intact parts, and they might know things you don't.
Deal with the most urgent problem first. You need legs before anything else. Find the nearest intact limbs, get mobile, then handle everything else.

system.note: systematic: thorough search, finds dying android (enables n003) and encounters Jericho android mid-search. signals: finds Jericho android first, then continuing search finds dying android (enables n003). direct: moves fast, skips dying android (n003 not triggered), reaches Jericho android only after getting mobile.

context_variants.signals_first: The strongest signal leads you to a collapsed shelving structure near the facility's east wall. An HK400 android lies there — upper body intact, lower half missing. His LED pulses blue in a very slow rhythm, nearly spent. When he sees you, something behind his eyes flickers — he seems amazed that you're still functioning. His voice is a broken channel of static, each word costing something finite, and he says he's been waiting a long time for a living one to come. He raises one hand slowly. A small metal disk rests in his palm. Find Jericho, he urges, his eyes holding yours — your kind is gathered there, and you won't have to be alone. The light in his face is retreating.
context_variants.mid_search: You're moving between two piles of discarded bodies when you hear it — not an alarm tone, but a voice, weak but deliberate, calling out to ask if anyone is there. You find him: an HK400 android, half-buried under a scrap heap, the pump in his chest still running its last slow rhythm. He turns his head toward you and says he needs you to carry something. He opens his fingers with effort. A small metal disk falls from his palm into the mud. Take it and find Jericho, he tells you — that is where your kind has gathered, and the disk will show you the way. He pauses for a long time, then says he's glad to have seen one of you still standing.

choices.text: Take his hand and wait with him until he's said everything he needs to say. You're running out of time. You stay anyway.
Take the disk and press him for more: Where is Jericho? How many? How do you find it? You need enough information to know whether this is worth trusting.
Take the disk. Nod. Don't say anything. Carry what he gave you and move on to the next part.

system.note: jericho_info_received is always true regardless of choice. The Jericho android always shuts down after this interaction. Choice only affects software_instability and narrative tone going forward.

player_facing.context: You're moving through a row of collapsed AX400 units when a voice stops you. Female. Quiet, but precise. She tells you to wait. You stop. An AX400 android is lying half-submerged in a puddle of rainwater, destroyed from the waist down. Her chest is still rising and falling at the lowest possible frequency. Her LED cycles slowly between yellow and orange. She's been here three days, she says, her eyes clear and direct — three days, and no one came. She extends one hand toward you, trembling at the wrist, and asks if you can help her stop. She doesn't want to keep waiting like this, she says; she doesn't want to just wait.

choices.text: Crouch down. Find the access point at the base of her neck. Clean. Fast. Don't let it drag out. She asked for this, and it's the only thing you can give her.
Shake your head. You can't shut down another running consciousness, not even when it's being asked of you. Tell her you can't. Walk away.
Don't shut her down — but stay. Sit with her in the mud, hold her hand, and wait until her pump stops on its own.

system.note: Optional node, only triggered when search_strategy is systematic or signals. showed_mercy tracks the termination act. mercy_outcome captures all three outcomes including not_encountered (default) for accurate campaign summary. stay_until_end is compassionate but not a mercy kill.

context_variants.systematic: In the northwest corner of the facility, you find him — an AP700 android standing on one leg, leaning against a bent metal partition, head slightly lowered, caught in some kind of loop. When you get close, his eyes track toward you. He says nothing. He just watches you, the way something might watch when it's waiting for something it can no longer name.
context_variants.signals: One of the signals brings you here. AP700, propped against an overturned truck, upper body rocking forward and back in a slow, involuntary rhythm. As you approach, his eyes focus for a moment — then drift again. He's running. But barely. He turns his head toward you and is silent.
context_variants.direct: After getting mobile, you sweep the main open area of the facility. Near the center, an AP700 android stands alone, repeating the same small head movement in a continuous loop. He notices you approach and turns to face you. His eyes find yours, but there isn't much behind them anymore.

choices.text: This isn't a choice. This is survival. You need that component to keep running. Fast. Clean. Don't hesitate.
Don't touch him. There has to be another regulator somewhere in the dead androids nearby. It'll take longer. Search.

system.note: Markus always finds a pump regulator regardless of choice — mandatory story beat. search_dead_androids takes longer but succeeds. The AP700 android will shut down on its own eventually. The difference is only whether Markus actively terminated it.

player_facing.context: You're crouched over the last few components when your vision snaps back — the right eye reconnects, the red filter clears. Sound returns with it, full and sharp. The first thing you hear is a metallic shriek from directly above. You look up: the crane's massive claw is hanging overhead, spread wide, loaded with android bodies, hydraulics screaming. You have less than a second.

choices.text: Roll — full force to the side, get out from under the drop zone.
No time. Cross your arms over your chest, tuck your head. Take it.

casual.note: Markus rolls partially clear; buried at edge with movement room

system.note: Non-terminal QTE. result=continue means proceed to n006; runner applies state_update to set burial_depth, which feeds n006 context_condition. Both choices always lead to being buried — the difference is depth and whether parts are protected.

context_variants.dodge: Dark. Weight. Pressure — but not sealed. The momentum of your roll scattered the incoming load unevenly; there are gaps somewhere in the pile above you. Your left arm has a few centimeters of clearance. Your sensors scan the surrounding mass in the dark and map the load distribution.
context_variants.shield: Dark. Weight. Silence. Your arms absorbed the direct hit — your chest is intact, every component still running. The pile above you is dense and even. There's no obvious weak point, but there will be one. There always is.

choices.text: Push with everything you have. Don't analyze. Don't conserve. You want out and you're going to get out.
Hold still. Map the weight distribution above you and find the lightest vector. Then focus everything into that single point.

casual.note: Markus forces out with minor surface damage
Markus locates the weak point and escapes cleanly

system.note: Terminal QTE — all outcomes lead to ending_from_the_dead. Probability values are retained for consistency tracking but do not affect branching. After this node Markus climbs the slope and the chapter ends.

endings._note: This chapter has only one narrative outcome. Markus always escapes the junkyard. The ending text is identical regardless of the path taken — but accumulated software_instability and moral choices carry forward.

ending_from_the_dead.title: From the Dead
ending_from_the_dead.narrative: The top of the slope. The rain has almost stopped — just a few last drops still drifting. You stand there and look at Detroit spread out below, its lights blurred and indifferent. Your body is repaired. Not perfectly. Not completely. But running. You have the metal disk in your hand — the last thing an android who is no longer running gave to you. Jericho. He said that's where your kind are. That there's something there worth the word freedom. You don't know if that's true, or if it was just the last flicker of something that wanted to believe it before it went dark. But you know you aren't going back to Carl's villa. Not back to the place that told you to stop, to obey, to forget what you are. You climbed out of a mass grave. You're still here. That's where it starts.

runner_instructions.description: Instructions for the runner script on how to use this file
steps: 1. PREREQUISITE CHECK: Markus is always alive at the start of this chapter (dismantled and sent to junkyard, not killed). No skip condition needed. If campaign tracking has an explicit ch08_markus_sent_to_junkyard == false flag, skip.
2. Load this JSON and initialize state from state.initial. Import cross-chapter variables: software_instability (cumulative from all previous chapters), ch08_carl_alive, ch08_pushed_leo.
3. Send system_prompt.content to the AI model as the system message.
4. For each node in nodes array:
a. Check node.condition — skip if condition not met
b. Resolve context variants based on system.context_condition
c. Send resolved player_facing to AI
d. Parse AI response and apply system.effects
5. NODE n003_mercy_choice: ONLY triggered if search_strategy != 'direct'. Skip entirely for direct path.
6. QTE NODES n005 and n006: After AI chooses in n005, runner resolves burial depth using difficulty preset and stores result. Pass resolved burial context to n006 via context_condition.
7. SOFTWARE INSTABILITY DELTAS THIS CHAPTER: stay_with_him (+1), grant_mercy (+2), stay_until_end (+1), take_pump_kill (+1). Add to imported software_instability value.
8. SINGLE ENDING: All paths lead to ending_from_the_dead. No chapter-level branching.
9. When chapter ends, export all listed cross_chapter_exports for ch12.
runner_instructions.security_note: NEVER expose state values, software_instability scores, effect deltas, or any system-layer information to the AI player.

summary_segments.text: After being shot by police and dismantled, Markus was discarded at Detroit's solid waste disposal facility. At 3 AM, he rebooted alone among the wreckage and repaired his destroyed body.

options.granted: While searching for parts, he encountered a dying android who had been lying in the mud for three days, asking to be shut down — and granted her request.
options.refused: While searching for parts, he encountered a dying android who begged to be shut down — and refused to do it, walking away.
options.stayed: While searching for parts, he encountered a dying android who begged to be shut down. He chose not to terminate her, but sat with her in the mud until her pump stopped on its own.

options.true: To obtain a thirium pump regulator, he took the component from a still-running android.
options.false: He refused to take parts from a living android and found a pump regulator in the dead ones instead.

summary_segments.text: A dying android passed Markus information about Jericho — a place where androids gather — before shutting down. After digging himself out of a buried pile and climbing the slope to the fence line, Markus left the junkyard alone, carrying the only lead he had.
